questions.question: A vertical pole of height $h$ meters stands on the ground. At a point $P$ on the ground, the angle of elevation of the top of the pole is $\alpha$. If the point $P$ is moved $d$ meters closer to the base of the pole, the angle of elevation becomes $\beta$. Express the height $h$ of the pole in terms of $d$, $\alpha$, and $\beta$.

steps: Let the pole be $AB = h$ and the initial distance $PB = x$. Using $\triangle ABP$, $\tan \alpha = h / x$, so $x = h / \tan \alpha$.
After moving $d$ meters closer, the new distance is $x - d$, and the angle is $\beta$. Using the new triangle, $\tan \beta = h / (x - d)$, which implies $x - d = h / \tan \beta$.
Subtract the two expressions for $x$ to eliminate it: $x - (x - d) = h / \tan \alpha - h / \tan \beta$.
Solve for $h$: $d = h (1 / \tan \alpha - 1 / \tan \beta) = h ((\tan \beta - \tan \alpha) / (\tan \alpha \tan \beta))$, leading to the final expression.
solution.derivation: $x = \frac{h}{\tan \alpha} \text{ and } x - d = \frac{h}{\tan \beta} \\ d = x - (x - d) = \frac{h}{\tan \alpha} - \frac{h}{\tan \beta} \\ d = h \left( \frac{\tan \beta - \tan \alpha}{\tan \alpha \tan \beta} \right) \\ h = \frac{d \tan \alpha \tan \beta}{\tan \beta - \tan \alpha}$
